name: Christy Cauble Davis
occupation: Business Owner (Davis Preservation, LLC) and Former USDA Rural Development State Director
education: B.A., Kansas State University (1997); Graduate degree, Wichita State University (1999)
family: Married to Luke Davis; one son, Jack
whoTheyAre:
Christy Cauble Davis is a fifth-generation Kansan from Cottonwood Falls, a town of about 900 people in the heart of the Flint Hills. She grew up in Sedgwick, graduated from Kansas State University in 1997, and earned a graduate degree from Wichita State University in 1999. She spent roughly 25 years working in historic preservation across all 105 Kansas counties before moving to Chase County in 2013 to lead Symphony in the Flint Hills, the annual outdoor concert series on the Tallgrass Prairie. She also owns Davis Preservation, LLC, and has served on the Chase County Chamber of Commerce board and as legislative chair of the Kansas Downtown Development Association.

In her personal life, Davis and her husband Luke have put their preservation philosophy into practice. In 2016, the couple adopted a historic building in Cottonwood Falls and converted it into an award-winning laundromat called Clean History, which was featured in American Coin-Op magazine. In 2019, they rehabilitated a historic bank building to create new housing and a bookstore. Both projects reflect her broader pitch to voters: that she invests in small-town Kansas with her own time and money, not just her words.

Her most prominent professional role came when President Biden appointed her Kansas State Director of USDA Rural Development. She held the position from 2022 until January 2025, overseeing more than $1.3 billion in federal investment across Kansas communities, including hospitals, clinics, nursing homes, housing developments, and community facilities. She also led a nationwide effort within USDA to reduce red tape in federal programs. That experience is the centerpiece of her Senate campaign.
theirRecord:
Davis has never held elected office. Her public record comes from appointed positions and nonprofit work rather than legislative votes. As USDA Rural Development State Director for Kansas, she managed a portfolio that touched every corner of the state, directing federal dollars into rural infrastructure, healthcare, and housing. That role gave her a concrete, dollar-figure talking point that most first-time candidates lack. Before that, her work in historic preservation took her to all 105 Kansas counties, giving her a statewide footprint built over decades of community-level engagement.

She ran for office once before. In 2020, she entered the Democratic primary for the Kansas 1st Congressional District and lost to Kali Barnett, who received roughly 63 percent of the vote. Barnett then lost to Republican Tracey Mann in the general election. That primary loss means Davis is not a total newcomer to campaigns, but it also means she has a track record of defeat in a competitive race.

The National Republican Senatorial Committee filed a complaint in March 2026 alleging that Davis and three other Democratic Senate candidates failed to file their required Personal Financial Disclosure Forms within 30 days of becoming candidates. This is a procedural filing issue rather than an allegation of corruption, but it handed Republicans a talking point about transparency. Her connection to the Biden administration is also expected to be a major line of attack in a state where Trump won by 14-plus points, even though the USDA role is the source of her strongest resume item.
whatYouShouldKnow: She is a fifth-generation Kansan who lives in Cottonwood Falls, population roughly 900, in the rural Flint Hills of Chase County.

She served as Kansas State Director of USDA Rural Development from 2022 to January 2025, overseeing more than $1.3 billion in federal investment across hospitals, housing, clinics, nursing homes, and community facilities statewide.
Her preservation work took her to all 105 Kansas counties over a 25-year career, giving her one of the broadest geographic footprints of any candidate in the race.
She and her husband Luke rehabilitated two historic buildings in Cottonwood Falls, converting one into a laundromat and another into housing and a bookstore.
She ran for the Kansas 1st Congressional District in 2020 and lost the Democratic primary to Kali Barnett with roughly 37 percent of the vote.
The NRSC filed a complaint in March 2026 alleging she was late filing a required Personal Financial Disclosure Form. This is a procedural matter, not an allegation of financial wrongdoing.
She was the first major Democrat to enter the 2026 Senate race, announcing in August 2025 with the slogan: 'It's time for Kansas to have a Senator who shows up.'
No public church affiliation has been identified in any available records or campaign materials.
whereTheyWorship: No church affiliation, congregation, or denomination has been publicly identified for Christy Cauble Davis. Her campaign materials and public interviews focus on rural Kansas, community investment, and federal service rather than personal faith or a specific place of worship. Multiple research passes did not turn up a verified church connection.

quotes.text: Kansans are uniquely equipped to tackle national challenges.
It's time for Kansas to have a Senator who shows up.
Rooted in Kansas. Ready to Rebuild.

campaignFinance.narrative: Davis raised roughly $80,000 through the end of 2025, spending about $38,100 and carrying $42,378 in cash on hand. Those numbers are modest by Senate standards and a fraction of incumbent Roger Marshall's $4.1 million war chest. No donor-by-donor breakdown was available in public reporting, so the composition of her fundraising base is not yet clear. The NRSC flagged her for a late Personal Financial Disclosure filing in March 2026, a procedural issue that generated negative headlines but does not suggest financial misconduct.
campaignFinance.undisclosed: No individual donor breakdown was available in the public FEC summaries reviewed. A full itemized report may become available after the next FEC filing deadline.
